content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1 0 S Street
Sacramento 95814
(916) 322-5660

November 4, 1975

Mr. Charles J. Sexton
Registrar of Voters
County of San Diego
P.O. Box 230, Port 4
San Diego, Ca. 92123

Re: Request for Opinion
No. 75-193

Dear Mr. Sexton:

Thank you for your letter of October 16, 19[?] requesting an opinion with regard to several questions under the Political Reform Act. Since your letter does not raise any substantial questions involving interpretation of the Act, your request for a formal opinion is denied. However, I hope the following informal comments will be helpful.

Your first question asked whether, for purposes of filing a Form 470, the limit of $200 received or expended during one's "candidacy" applies to both elections combined.

If the candidate files a postelection report no later than 65 days following the primary election, he is considered to be a candidate in a separate election for the general election. The $200 limit then applies to each election separately. However, if the general election is held within 60 days of the primary, the candidate is not required to file a postelection statement. Rather, pursuant to Section 84201 of the Act, he is required to file three preelection statements: two before the primary election and one before the general election. His "candidacy" in this situation extends and ends through both elections. Thus if the general election is held within 60 days of the primary election, a candidate may file short Form 470 only if he raises and spends no more than $200 for both elections combined.

In question number two, you asked whether the term "cumulative amount" in a general election report is the

Mr. Charles J. Sexton
November 4, 1975
Page Two

amount contributed or expended since the closing date of
the posteliminary statement filed by the candidate of does
the term "cumulative amount" include both the primary and
general elections. If the candidate has filed a postelection
report following the primary election, then he begins
cumulative amounts from the closing date of his posteelection
report. As indicated above, if the general election is held
within 60 days after the primary election, no posteelection
report is filed after the primary. In this situation, the
cumulative amount includes both the primary and the general
elections.

In question number three, you asked if an elected
official is required to file a Form 430 off-year statement
every six months during his entire tenure in office if he
expended or received no more than $200 for his campaign for
that office. Section 84206 of the Act requires an elected
officer to file an off-year statement every six months unless the elected officer is a judge or has a salary of less
than $100 a month. If the elected officer received contributions or made expenditures of less than $200 since his
last postelection statement, he may file a short Form 470;
however, if he received or spent more than $200, then he
must file a Form 430. Thus, he may file a short Form 470
if he raised or spent less than $200 since his previous
election.

Finally, we would like to observe that the questions
you raised in your letter are questions which involve the
duties of a candidate rather than the duties of a county
clerk or registrar of voters. Although we understand that,
undoubtedly, you are asked questions like those you have posed
and, therefore, need answers in order to be able to respond,
the Act authorizes the Commission to issue an opinion only
to a "person . . . with respect to his duties" under the Act.
Cal. Gov. Code Section 83112 (emphasis added). Accordingly,
when you receive questions regarding a candidate's obligations, it would be preferable to suggest that the candidate
who asks the questions submit the opinion request to the
Commission.

If you have any further questions, please contact
Natalie West, an attorney on the Commission staff.

Sincerely,

Daniel H. Lowenstein
Chairman, for the Commission

COUNTY OF SAN DIEGO
REGISTRAR OF VOTERS

1555 OVERLAND AVENUE
P.O. BOX 2301-I
SAN DIEGO, CALIFORNIA
92123
(714) 565-5513

COMMUNITY SERVICES AGENCY

Request No. [illegible] 193
Date Received 10-20-75
Response Due 11-3-75

CHARLES J. SEXTON
REGISTRAR OF VOTERS

October 16, 1975

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Gentlemen:

This office would like an opinion on the following questions:

The Political Reform Act of 1974 defines Sec. 82022: "Election means any
primary, general, special or recall election held in this state. The
primary and general or special elections are separate elections for purposes of this title".

1. Is a candidate who qualifies in the primary and then is a candidate in
the general election considered to be a candidate in separate elections?
For the purposes of using FPPC Form 470, is "entire campaign" each
election separately or the two together (primary and general)? Is
"support of my candidacy" the primary and general campaign or is
each candidacy a separate one for the separate elections?

2. Is the "cumulative amount," Section 82018, in a general election report
the amount contributed or expended since "the closing date of the past
primary statement filed by the candidate or does "cumulative amount"
include both primary and general election contributions and expenditures?

3. Is an elected officer required to file a 439 campaign statement "off
year" every six months during his entire tenure in office if he expended
or received more than $200 in his campaign for that office?

Very truly yours,

[Signature]
CHARLES J. SEXTON
Registrar of Voters
